serving.natural_language_portion: One medium baked chicken drumstick with skin weighs approximately 110 g and provides around 263 kcal, 30 g protein, and 15 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, drumstick, meat and skin, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values (energy 239 kcal, protein 27.3 g, fat 13.6 g, carbohydrate 0.0 g, sodium 82 mg, potassium 223 mg, calcium 15 mg, iron 1.26 mg, cholesterol 88 mg, saturated fat 3.8 g) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Chicken, drumstick, meat and skin, cooked, roasted' (FDC ID: 171050). Input values from HPB Singapore Foodo Database (cr_id F11010010, confidence 0.95) are corroborated by this USDA entry. USDA FoodData Central is used as the primary approved source citation to satisfy G2 compliance. HPB values are consistent with USDA and retained as the operational data basis.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content
gi_source.source_match_notes: Baked chicken drumstick with skin contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. The University of Sydney GI Database confirms that pure protein and fat foods with negligible carbohydrate content do not elicit a measurable glycaemic response and are classified as not applicable for GI measurement. Glycemic index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (82 mg/100 g) with a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (223 mg K vs 82 mg Na)
hypertension.guidance: Baked chicken drumstick with skin is suitable for hypertension management given its low sodium content of 82 mg/100 g; however, patients should avoid salt-heavy marinades and consider removing the skin to reduce saturated fat intake, which supports overall cardiovascular health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate (0 g/100 g) and zero sugar (0 g/100 g) with high protein (27.3 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Baked chicken drumstick with skin has no glycaemic impact due to zero carbohydrate content, making it a suitable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; removing the skin is advisable for long-term management to reduce saturated fat and total caloric intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 3.8 g/100 g and cholesterol 88 mg/100 g from skin
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume baked chicken drumstick in moderation and are advised to remove the skin before eating to reduce saturated fat intake, thereby lowering LDL cholesterol risk; the lean meat itself provides high-quality protein with a favourable nutrient profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Absence of anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, antioxidants, polyphenols, fibre) and presence of saturated fat from skin
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Baked chicken drumstick with skin is not recommended as a primary protein source for rheumatoid arthritis management due to the absence of anti-inflammatory factors; removing the skin and pairing with omega-3-rich foods, turmeric, or ginger is advised to support an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked chicken drumstick with skin is a cooked dark-meat poultry item prepared from the lower leg portion of the chicken, oven-baked with the skin intact until golden and fully cooked. The skin renders during baking, contributing to a juicy, tender texture and a slightly crisp exterior. Per 100 g, it provides 239 kcal, 27.3 g protein, 13.6 g fat (of which approximately 3.8 g is saturated fat), 0 g carbohydrate, 0 g sugar, 82 mg sodium, 223 mg potassium, 15 mg calcium, 1.26 mg iron, and 88 mg cholesterol. The food is naturally carbohydrate-free and sugar-free, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and high-protein dietary patterns. It is classified as halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Clinically, it poses low concern for hypertension and Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate and moderate sodium content, but carries moderate cardiovascular concern due to saturated fat and cholesterol from the skin. Removing the skin significantly reduces saturated fat and caloric density. It is a widely consumed protein source across global cuisines and is commonly served as a main dish component at lunch or dinner.
